draft.introduction: Confessions Book 7 narrates Augustine’s encounter with Neoplatonic philosophy, which freed him from Manichaean materialism and enabled him to conceive of God as incorporeal. It describes a failed mystical ascent that nonetheless prepared him for Christian conversion.

translation.text: And being thence admonished to return to myself, I entered into my own depths, with you as my guide. I entered and saw with the eye of my soul, above that same eye of my soul, above my mind, the unchangeable light—not this common light visible to all flesh. It was above because it made me, and I was below because I was made by it.
translation.original_text: Et inde admonitus redire ad memet ipsum intravi in intima mea duce te et potui

translator_notes.note: Augustine’s vision of the “unchangeable light” (lux incommutabilis) follows the Plotinian pattern of inward ascent but differs crucially: the light is not the soul’s own depth but something transcendent that made the soul. This distinction separates Christian mysticism from Neoplatonic self-discovery.
